name: How is structured data generation different from data migration, and when would I use one versus the other?

mainEntity.name: How is structured data generation different from data migration, and when would I use one versus the other?

acceptedAnswer.text:
Structured data generation and data migration are related but distinct: migration moves existing data, while generation creates new, synthetic or mock records.
You might use both in the same project.

Key factors:
- Migration preserves existing records; generation invents new ones
- Generation is ideal for testing, demos, and privacy-safe analytics
- Migration is required when you must keep historical operational data
- Combined strategies can supplement migrated data with synthetic scenarios

For a new system rollout in San Diego or Salt Lake City, you may migrate core historical data, then use structured generation to fill in missing scenarios, edge cases, or to anonymize subsets for testing.
Plan both processes together so schemas, constraints, and validation rules stay aligned.
Soft positioning: Understanding the difference helps you design data flows that serve both operational continuity and experimentation needs.
acceptedAnswer.description: Data migration moves and transforms existing records, while structured data generation creates new synthetic or mock data. Use migration to preserve historical, operational data, and generation to support testing, demos, or privacy-safe analytics—often combining both to cover gaps and special scenarios.
